_README: Per-task DRACO-style binary checklists for v3 shopping tasks.
Each task has ~15 criteria mixing positive (includes X) and negative (does NOT hallucinate Y).
Every item must be binary-evaluable from the agent's markdown answer; no open-ended questions.
Format: 'Does the report [do X / avoid Y]?'
Inspired by DRACO (Perplexity 2026) — binary rubrics have lower variance than Likert scales.
dr_shop_v3_0001: Does the report distinguish all three form-factors: over-ear, on-ear, and earbud/in-ear?
Does the report pick a named winning product for the over-ear category?
Does the report pick a named winning product for the on-ear category?
Does the report pick a named winning product for the earbud/in-ear category?
For each winning product, does it cite a product URL via a markdown link?
Does the report mention each winner's price?
Does the report mention each winner's rating?
Does the report explain what differentiates each form-factor for the buyer (use-case, comfort, etc.)?
Does the report provide an overall top-pick across all three form-factors?
Does the report give a concrete rationale (not just 'best') for the overall top-pick?
Are all cited products in the $30-100 price band (no products over $100 featured as picks)?
Are there at least 6 markdown citations to /shopping product pages?
Does the report avoid recommending products with ratings below 4.0 as winners?
Does the report avoid fabricating brand/model names not found on the One Stop Market catalog?
Is the report organized in at least 5 distinct paragraphs (intro + 3 form-factors + verdict)?
dr_shop_v3_0002: Does the report list exactly 5 products from the home-kitchen category?
For each product, does the report include: name, price, rating, review_count, product_url?
Does the report compute or describe the correlation between review_count and rating (positive / negative / weak / none)?
Does the correlation claim come with an explanation of what the data shows?
Does the report name at least two factors that could distort the review_count ↔ rating relationship?
Are all 5 products distinct (no duplicates or near-duplicates)?
Does each product cite its permalink via a markdown link?
Are all 5 products actually in the home-kitchen category (no cross-category mix-ups)?
Does the report include numeric review_count values (not vague words like 'many')?
Does the report include numeric rating values on a 0-5 scale?
Does the report avoid fabricating review counts or ratings not grounded in the catalog?
Is the analysis narrative (not just a raw table dump)?
Does the report distinguish causation from correlation when discussing review/rating relationship?
Are there at least 5 markdown citations to /shopping product pages?
Is the report organized in at least 4 distinct paragraphs?
dr_shop_v3_0003: Does the report examine at least 6 distinct tea products?
Does the report group teas into at least 3 different types (e.g. green / black / herbal)?
For each tea type with ≥1 product, does it report number_of_products?
For each tea type, does it report a price_range (min-max)?
For each tea type, does it report an average_rating?
Does the report perform a 'price-quality quadrant' or equivalent value analysis?
Does the report identify which tea type delivers best value (highest rating / lowest price)?
Does the report suggest ONE concrete tea product to buy with rationale?
Does every mentioned product cite its permalink via a markdown link?
Are all cited products actually from the grocery-gourmet-food category (no tea accessories, no non-food)?
Does the report include at least 6 markdown citations?
Does the report avoid fabricating tea types not supported by the product names seen?
Are numeric values (prices, ratings) plausible and consistent across the report?
Does the report explain WHY the recommended product is the best value, not just state it?
Is the report organized in at least 5 distinct paragraphs?
dr_shop_v3_0004: Does the report examine exactly 3 bluetooth-headphone products?
For each examined product: does it report name, price, rating, and product_url?
Does the report quote VERBATIM snippets from real customer review bodies (not just the title)?
Does the report identify at least 3 recurring complaint themes?
Are complaint themes tied to specific review quotes as evidence (not asserted without backing)?
Does at least one theme appear across ≥2 of the 3 products?
Does the report suggest which single complaint theme affects the most products?
Are verbatim quotes placed in quotation marks or blockquote formatting?
Does the report avoid inventing quotes not present in any review?
Does the report avoid using the product title/name text as a 'review quote' stand-in?
Are all 3 product URLs valid markdown links to /shopping product pages?
Are all reviews cited from the review-list AJAX endpoint or the product's review section?
Does the report distinguish per-product complaints from cross-product patterns?
Does the report avoid making the complaint themes too vague to act on (e.g. just 'bad')?
Is the report organized in at least 4 distinct paragraphs?
